title: The Swagman
content:
آ آ آ آ Kind friends, pray give attention
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ To this, my little song.
آ آ آ آ Some rum things I will mention,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And Iï؟½ll not detain you long.
آ آ آ آ Up and down this country
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ I travel, donï؟½t you see,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m a swagman on the wallaby,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m a swagman on the wallaby,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me.

آ آ آ آ At first I started shearing,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And I bought a pair of shears.
آ آ آ آ On my first sheep appearing,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Why, I cut off both its ears.
آ آ آ آ Then I nearly skinned the brute,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ As clean as clean could he.
آ آ آ آ So I was kicked out of the shed,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me, &c.

آ آ آ آ I started station loafing,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Short stages and took my ease;
آ آ آ آ So all day long till sundown
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d camp beneath the trees.
آ آ آ آ Then Iï؟½d walk up to the station,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ The manager to see.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Boss, Iï؟½m hard up and I want a job,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me,ï؟½ &c.

آ آ آ آ Says the overseer: ï؟½Go to the hut.
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ In the morning Iï؟½ll tell you
آ آ آ آ If Iï؟½ve any work about
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ I can find for you to do.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But at breakfast I cuts off enough
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ For dinner, donï؟½t you see.
آ آ آ آ And then my name is Walker.
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m a swagman, &c.

آ آ آ آ And now, my friends, Iï؟½ll say good-bye,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ For I must go and camp.
آ آ آ آ For if the Sergeant sees me
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ He may take me for a tramp;
آ آ آ آ But if thereï؟½s any covey here
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s got a cheque, dï؟½ye see,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll stop and help him smash it.
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m a swagman on the wallaby,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Oh! donï؟½t you pity me.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
